summary: Early Netherlandish painter, leading painter in Bruges after Jan van Eyck's death (alongside Hans Memling). Most commonly identified by Portrait of a Carthusian (trompe l'oeil fly) and A Goldsmith in His Shop (convex mirror, possibly depicting St. Eligius). Introduced geometric perspective to Flemish painting.

works.name: Portrait of a Carthusian (1446)
works.description: Portrait of a fork-bearded monk against a red background. Famous for a trompe l'oeil fly painted on the frame. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A trompe l'oeil fly sits on the frame; man with forked beard against red background
examples: included a trompe l'oeil fly in his Portrait of a Carthusian
A fly sits on the trompe-l'oeil frame of a portrait by this artist

works.name: A Goldsmith in His Shop (1449)
works.description: Scene of a goldsmith (possibly St. Eligius) weighing a wedding ring for a couple. Features a convex mirror showing two men. Shelf with piece of coral. Red girdle trails over windowsill. Influenced Quentin Massys.

clues.clue: Man (possibly St. Eligius) weighs wedding ring for wealthy couple; convex mirror shows two men; shelf with coral
examples: a man who might be Saint Eligius weighs a wedding ring for a young, wealthy couple
features a shelf with a piece of coral and was once believed to depict St. Eligius
influenced Quentin Massys with a genre scene featuring a convex mirror

examples: the title figure's soul is shown being carried to heaven by miniature angels

clues.clue: Borrowed Van Eyck's foreshortened skeleton-flying-over-Hell for his own Last Judgment, with scarier chiaroscuro Hell
examples: borrowed Jan van Eyck's foreshortened-skeleton-flying-over-Hell image from the Last Judgment

recursive_suggestions: Jan van Eyck — predecessor whose workshop he possibly inherited
comprehensive_summary:
Petrus Christus was an Early Netherlandish painter who was the leading artist in Bruges after Jan van Eyck's death, alongside Hans Memling. His Portrait of a Carthusian is famous for a trompe l'oeil fly painted on the frame, below a fork-bearded monk against a red background. A Goldsmith in His Shop shows a man (possibly St. Eligius) weighing a wedding ring for a wealthy couple, with a convex mirror showing two men and a shelf with coral. It influenced Quentin Massys.

Christus pioneered geometric perspective and the vanishing point in Northern European painting. His Madonna of the Dry Tree features 15 gilt letters 'A' (Ave Maria) hanging from thorny branches. His Portrait of a Young Girl is side-eyed and influenced by Rogier van der Weyden. He possibly inherited Van Eyck's workshop. His Death of the Virgin shows the title figure's soul carried by miniature angels. His Head of Christ at the Met adds blood dripping from the Crown of Thorns to a lost Van Eyck composition.
